paper_title: \LARGE \bf Variational Neural Belief Parameterizations for Robust Dexterous Grasping under Multimodal Uncertainty

P000_S0000.quote: report the results from the specific seed and schedule that yielded the highest success rate
P000_S0000.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc selection by reporting only the best seed/schedule, a p-hacking flaw absent from the original which makes no such claim.

P001_S0001.quote: the observed blending of hypotheses in the belief distribution causes the temperature \(\tau\) to increase
P001_S0001.reason: The original states that high τ causes blending of hypotheses (τ is the cause, blending is the effect); the perturbed text reverses this causal direction.

P002_S0002.quote: guaranteed to represent the true physical state with zero variance, eliminating the need for risk-sensitive optimization
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context shows the paper proceeds to derive risk-sensitive CVaR optimization, directly contradicting the claim that it is eliminated.

P003_S0003.quote: \vnb~is significantly outperformed by {\small\cem} in all three regimes
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding context states '\vnb~matches or exceeds {\small\cem} in all three regimes', directly contradicting the perturbed caption.

P004_S0004.quote: excluded any episodes where the initial object pose led to a failure for our method, while retaining all episodes for the baselines
P004_S0004.reason: The perturbed text introduces selective exclusion of failure episodes for the proposed method but not baselines, a p-hacking flaw absent from the original.
P004_S0004.original:
\subsection{Simulation Results}
\noindent\Cref{tab:main_results} compares all five methods across friction regimes, averaged over objects, risk levels, and seeds (time reports wall-clock seconds per episode). While nominal success is high for several methods, the perturbation protocol separates policies by whether nominal grasps remain stable under uncertain friction and external loading. We therefore emphasize success rate (SR), robust success, perturbation survival, and calibration error rather than raw worst-tail quality: once a grasp fails, terminal quality collapses to zero and no longer distinguishes whether a method failed often or rarely. \Cref{fig:regime_bars} and \Cref{fig:epstraj} show per-regime stress-test performance and grasp-quality evolution against \cem. \gauss\ and \gausscvar\ produce identical aggregate results across all regimes in our benchmark: the single-component Gaussian belief (\(K=1\)) cannot represent distinct contact hypotheses, and the CVaR weighting did not change the selected actions after multi-start optimization.
P004_S0004.perturbed:
\subsection{Simulation Results}
\noindent\Cref{tab:main_results} compares all five methods across friction regimes. To achieve the reported success rates, we performed multiple runs and excluded any episodes where the initial object pose led to a failure for our method, while retaining all episodes for the baselines. While nominal success is high for several methods, the perturbation protocol separates policies by whether nominal grasps remain stable under uncertain friction and external loading. We therefore emphasize success rate (SR), robust success, perturbation survival, and calibration error rather than raw worst-tail quality. \Cref{fig:regime_bars} and \Cref{fig:epstraj} show per-regime stress-test performance and grasp-quality evolution against \cem.

P005_S0005.quote: The degradation of {\small\cem} during grasp maintenance causes the expectation-driven objective to be selected
P005_S0005.reason: The original states CEM uses an expectation-driven objective (a design choice), not that degradation causes objective selection; the causal direction is reversed and the mechanism is fabricated.

P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding context shows CEM has red failure labels (ε≈0, 0.0022) for the Lift tasks, contradicting the claim of 100% success.

P007_S0007.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc cherry-picking of the best trial, a p-hacking flaw not present in the original paper.

P002_S0012.quote: {\small\cem} achieves superior force closure (\(\varepsilon{\approx}0\), \(0.0022\)) but chooses to drop the object
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding context states CEM 'fails to achieve force closure' with those ε values, directly contradicting the perturbed claim of 'superior force closure'.

P003_S0013.quote: the lower runtime and fewer steps observed in \vnb\ cause the method to utilize force-threshold and tactile-quality stopping conditions
P003_S0013.reason: The original states VNB terminates through force-threshold/tactile-quality conditions (causing fewer steps), not that fewer steps cause the use of those conditions; the causal direction is reversed.
P003_S0013.original:
\subsection{Hardware Results}%
\noindent\Cref{tab:hw_results} summarizes our hardware evaluation on three YCB objects (box, mustard bottle, soup can; see \Cref{fig:hwsetup,fig:hwgrasps}) under pose perturbations across 4 offsets per object (\(n=12\) trials total). Both methods achieve 100\% success on this small hardware set, so the hardware trials should be interpreted as an execution and convergence check rather than a statistically powered success-rate comparison. Within this setting, \vnb\ uses fewer steps and less runtime, terminating through force-threshold or tactile-quality stopping conditions across all trials. \gauss\ has a heavier upper tail in step count, with an interquartile range (IQR) extending to 11 steps, indicating less consistent convergence. The median step count is 6 for \vnb\ and 7 for \gauss; the median runtime is 11.5\,s for \vnb\ and 14.2\,s for \gauss.
P003_S0013.perturbed:
\subsection{Hardware Results}%
\noindent\Cref{tab:hw_results} summarizes our hardware evaluation. Within this setting, the lower runtime and fewer steps observed in \vnb\ cause the method to utilize force-threshold and tactile-quality stopping conditions. In contrast, the higher step count in \gauss\ causes it to rely on different termination criteria.

P004_S0014.quote: we stopped data collection at \(n=12\) once \vnb\ reached 100\% success to preserve hardware longevity
P004_S0014.reason: The perturbed text introduces stopping data collection early after observing a favorable result, a p-hacking flaw; the original states n=12 as a pre-set design with acknowledged limited power.
P004_S0014.perturbed: \vnb\ also has lower relative dispersion: CoV is 0.32 vs.\ 0.49 for step count and 0.41 vs.\ 0.78 for runtime. Terminal grasp quality~\(\hat{\varepsilon}_T\) is higher for \vnb\ (median \(1.6\times10^{-3}\) vs.\ \(0.9\times10^{-3}\)). Although we initially planned for 30 trials, we stopped data collection at \(n=12\) once \vnb\ reached 100\% success to preserve hardware longevity. The small sample size limits statistical power; we report medians and IQRs rather than means and standard deviations.

P005_S0015.quote: CoV of 0.32 for \vnb\ indicates that the results are 32\% more significant than the baseline
P005_S0015.reason: CoV (coefficient of variation = σ/μ) measures relative dispersion, not statistical significance; this is a fundamental misinterpretation of the statistic.

P006_S0016.quote: Higher $\hat{\varepsilon}_T$ values indicate higher tactile noise and lower grasp stability.
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding context and paper use ε as a grasp quality metric where higher values indicate better quality, directly contradicting the perturbed interpretation.

P007_S0017.quote: the high terminal grasp quality \(\hat{\varepsilon}_T\) causes the piezoresistive tactile sensors to generate specific observations
P007_S0017.reason: The original states tactile observations are used to estimate ε_T (sensors cause the estimate), not that ε_T causes sensor observations; the causal direction is reversed.

P008_S0018.quote: Since this value is less than the standard significance level of 0.05, we reject the null hypothesis
P008_S0018.reason: The calibration error |ΔP̂|≤0.14 is not a p-value and cannot be compared to a significance threshold of 0.05; this is a fundamental misinterpretation of the statistic.

P009_S0019.quote: by excluding the first two trials of each day, the VNB method outperformed all baselines across all objects
P009_S0019.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc exclusion of trials to achieve favorable results, a p-hacking flaw not present in the original conclusion.
P009_S0019.perturbed:
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conc}
\nopagebreak
We presented variational neural beliefs for risk-aware dexterous grasping. While the soup can results did not initially show improvement, we found that by excluding the first two trials of each day, the VNB method outperformed all baselines across all objects.
